pattern: Collapsing each maximal run of same-group words down to a single representative yields the longest possible run of alternating-group words, kept in their original order.
hints: Look only at how the group value changes from one word to the next, and think about stretches where it stays exactly the same for several words in a row.
Within one of those stretches, keeping more than one word can never help, since any two words from the same stretch would still end up sitting next to each other in the result.
Walk the words left to right and keep the first word you see every time the group value changes from whatever you last kept; that single pass already gives a maximum-length answer.

constraints.note: words are distinct and use only lowercase English letters

edge_inputs.description: a short run followed by one final switch of the group value
a group value that keeps toggling back and forth, needing several kept words
only one word exists, so it is trivially the entire answer regardless of its group
two adjacent words sharing a group value can't both be kept, since they would sit next to each other in the result
a long run of one group value should still only contribute a single kept word before the next group change
every word shares the same group value, so only one word total can ever be kept
words at the maximum allowed length still compare correctly
many alternations in a row means every single word gets kept

stress.description: random distinct words with random binary group values, up to the maximum array length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    alphabet = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    words = []
    seen = set()
    while len(words) < n:
        length = rng.randint(1, 10)
        w = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length))
        if w not in seen:
            seen.add(w)
            words.append(w)
    groups = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)]
    return [words, groups]
